metadata.title:
HITCHCOCK THR0TTLES THIEVES


WHO STEAL THE PUBLIC LANDS

pages.raw_text:
WARRINGTON, D. c. Dec. Ill
AS watchdog of the public domain,
Ethan Allen Hitchcock, who is soon
toAfetire as secretary of the in
teribr,has prosecuted more crimi-
mails and saved more dollars to the
people than any 10 public officials
during the past so years.


It has been a story of the clash
of the greatest political bowers
with the utmost of the govern
ments strength exerted in an en-
heavor to make law apply equally
to the great as we'll as the small.


Secretary Hitchcock is a man of
few words and of conservative
statement, but what he says is his
official statement of this matter:


"Every embarrassment that hui
man ingenuity could devise was put
in the way of my endeavors to re-
lease the public domain from the
grip of it's despolers," and he adds:
Powerful influences have been con-
cerned, and have not hesitated to
aggressively exert every agency
that could be commanded to weak
en- the hand of the law"


Tt is an open secret that the pow
erful influences" mentioned by
the secretary are United States
senators, members Of congress,
land Office officials and federal
judges.


The activity of the secretary of
the interior has resulted in:


Persons indicted, 400. .....


Persons convicted, SO.


Indictments still pending, 401.


The enormous labor involved in
the protection of the public lands
may be judged from the fact that
there are no less than 70 special
agents constantly travelling about
the country investigating fraudu-
lent entries, trespass and illegal ap-
preparation. Now there are 1914
entries and filings waiting to be
passed upon.


During the year just closed 324
cases of timber stealing were re-
ported to the department. In GO
cases the department decided to
bring suit to recover damages, and
propositions for settlement were ac-
